source.seedLabel: Alex Wakefield (CRO, AcuityMD) on his Ops→CEO→CRO arc, scaling vertical SaaS, and AI-first GTM

topics.title: The CEO-to-CRO Downgrade That Isn't
topics.angle: Going from CEO back to CRO is not a step down — when product-market fit and equity math line up, it's the highest-leverage seat in the building. Most operators chase the title; the smart ones chase the build.
topics.sourceQuote: I talked to no fewer than 25 people... 'Is this kind of crazy to do this move from CEO to CRO?'... when you're just kind of building and you can drive exponential growth, a lot of the, you know, honestly, like compensation, equity, and how you feel about yourself, math, just kind of makes sense when you're in a good build in a high growth mode.

topics.title: Late RevOps Is the Tax You Pay Forever
topics.angle: Most CROs admit they bring in RevOps too late. The cost isn't just bad dashboards — it's compounding bad hiring, bad enablement, and bad territory decisions made on no data for 18+ months.
topics.sourceQuote: We didn't bring RevOps on until maybe three years, two and a half years after I joined or something like that. And it was a lot of catch up. It was a lot of catch up.

topics.title: The 6-8-10 Rep Rule for RevOps
topics.angle: A simple trigger for when to hire RevOps: as soon as you have 6-10 reps generating data, you need someone whose full-time job is making that data trustworthy. Before that, the founder/CRO can stitch it. After, you can't catch up.
topics.sourceQuote: I think that as soon as you have some sort of critical mass where you've got enough reps, whatever you want to call it, like six, eight, ten, whatever the number is, where you need to start collecting data and assessing the efficacy of what is working that they're doing.

topics.title: Hire RevOps for Dirt, Not Dashboards
topics.angle: The RevOps job ad needs to say 'data janitor' loud and clear. The best RevOps hires want to get their hands dirty in CRM hygiene, not build a Looker dashboard from day one.
topics.sourceQuote: I know RevOps sounds super exciting, but I hope you want to get your hands dirty in like data cleanliness, because we have to build that foundation in order to build from there.

topics.title: Don't Lay Off — Just Don't Over-Hire
topics.angle: Financially conservative GTM orgs don't fire well; they hire slowly. The discipline is in setting ramped-quota thresholds and pulling back hiring the moment attainment slips, not waiting for a layoff event.
topics.sourceQuote: We maintain a really strong headcount plan, but we want to try to make it streamlined. So we do a good job, I think, of not hiring rather than letting people go.

topics.title: Over-Hiring Kills Your A-Players Faster Than Layoffs
topics.angle: The worst consequence of over-hiring isn't bloated payroll — it's territory dilution that drives your top reps out the door. And when A-players leave, the market notices instantly.
topics.sourceQuote: If you have A player reps that start to leave, people notice that. That that is about the worst thing that can happen.

topics.title: Top 5 Paid = Top 5 Reps. That's the Test.
topics.angle: Healthy GTM orgs share one trait: the top 5 paid people in the company are the top 5 sales reps. If it's anyone else, your incentive structure is broken.
topics.sourceQuote: If you're if you're top five raps or the top five paid people in your company, you're in a good spot.

topics.title: Vertical SaaS Hiring: Mirror Your Customer
topics.angle: In vertical SaaS, the best hires aren't the slickest SaaS sellers — they're people whose work ethic mirrors your customers'. If your buyers grind, you need grinders. If they're mission-driven, hire for mission alignment, not pedigree.
topics.sourceQuote: I always like to try to mirror emulate your customer base... Does someone really want to put in the effort to become an expert and support customers that work really hard and actually have a really great altruistic mission.

topics.title: The Three Stages of GTM Build
topics.angle: $2M to $50M ARR isn't one motion — it's three: hand-to-hand combat (0-10M), process layering (10-25M), and pipeline-metric sophistication (25M+). Most founders try to install stage-three rigor in a stage-one company.
topics.sourceQuote: It was an interesting stage because I think a lot of that like in the in the mud fighting and just growing like your pipeline and winning deals through excitement was really f great and took us from, you know, 20 customers to 100... But then you do have to pivot.

topics.title: The 20% Pickup Rate Most Teams Will Never Get
topics.angle: AcuityMD gets 20% phone pickup rates and 12-13% email response rates because they sell vertical and curate every message. Spray-and-pray outbound is dead for everyone except the lowest-stakes commodity sale.
topics.sourceQuote: We have response rates, we have pickup rates on dials of like twenty percent. It's absurd... Response rates on emails are very high, 12%, 13%, something like that.

topics.title: Curated > Volume in 2026 Outbound
topics.angle: Specific research + use-case-matched messaging + a 90-second Loom recorded inside the product beats a 2% blast every time. The math now favors quality so heavily it's not even close.
topics.sourceQuote: I I've I've always been more of a fan of really thoughtful curated messaging... Quick looms, you know, quick videos, 90 seconds specific to our customer, built in our software, sent to them in a video.

topics.title: Reps Should Generate 50% of Their Own Pipe
topics.angle: Stop blaming SDRs and marketing for thin pipeline. The benchmark for a healthy field org is reps self-sourcing roughly half their pipeline — and your best reps will always outperform that number.
topics.sourceQuote: I think reps are gonna generate fifty percent of their primary pipeline, whatever, give or take... more successful reps definitely create more of their own pipe.

topics.title: Events Are Back — But Only If You Run Them Like a Sales Cycle
topics.angle: Events stopped being boondoggles the moment teams started running pre-cycle, on-site execution, and post-event accountability tracked in CRM. The magic isn't the booth — it's the operational wrap-around.
topics.sourceQuote: There's tons of planning preps, precep meetings, tons of follow-up and and and holding folks accountable to communications and tracking that through CRM to make sure things are coming to closure.

topics.title: The AI-First Mental Wall
topics.angle: The blocker to AI adoption isn't tech literacy — it's people clinging to the idea that software is hard. AI doesn't add a new wave to learn; it removes the dropdowns, menus, and tabs you used to memorize.
topics.sourceQuote: AI actually makes it a ton easier to interact with technology. It actually like brings it backwards. Don't overthink it. Ask whatever you want.

topics.title: Decentralize Experimentation, Centralize Production
topics.angle: Let every employee build their own AI hacks for daily work. But the moment something becomes a process the org depends on, RevOps owns it. Anything else creates a bowl of spaghetti within six months.
topics.sourceQuote: I'm trying to move it more into RevOps centralized to say, hey, look, like here's a skill you can use... rather than they build it on their own.

topics.title: The Spaghetti Phase Is a Real Phase
topics.angle: Every company giving employees Claude Code is going through the same arc: explosion of creativity → bowl of spaghetti → forced consolidation. Skipping the spaghetti phase is impossible; shortening it is the game.
topics.sourceQuote: Everybody started getting their hands on Claude Code here. And it was just like a massive explosion of creativity and building things. And then it quickly turned into a bowl of spaghetti and chaos.

topics.title: Pivot to AI-Native Means Eating Some Growth
topics.angle: The honest tradeoff in becoming AI-native: you can rip the bandage off and get killed on revenue this year, or you can slow-burn the transition and protect growth. There's no third option.
topics.sourceQuote: Are you gonna do this like right away and get killed in revenue? Or are you gonna slow burn it a little bit and continue like a really nice healthy growth?

topics.title: Your Customer-Facing Team Must Be Power Users of Your Own AI
topics.angle: If you've shipped AI features in your product, your CSMs, AEs, and SEs need to use them every day. The Sherpa model — internal team teaches the customer how to use it — is how you actually drive adoption past the launch press release.
topics.sourceQuote: We are now ensuring that our team, our customer facing team, which is about, I don't know, 80 or 90 people, but our customer facing team is an expert in our own AI solutions.

topics.title: GTM-Side AI Adoption Is Outrunning Eng/Product
topics.angle: Counter to every dev-team-first assumption, RevOps and CS teams are adopting AI faster than engineering. Why? Less muscle memory to unlearn. The teams without legacy workflows leapfrog the ones with them.
topics.sourceQuote: They've noticed across their portfolio, there has been much higher adoption on the go-to-market side of AI in general than on the product and engineering side... we have go to market engineers, mostly heads down... half technical, but and they've adopted AI a little bit more than our engineering team did.

topics.title: Selling Is Just Project Management With Psychology On Top
topics.angle: The operators-turned-sellers who outperform realize this: closing a complex deal is a project plan with stakeholder management bolted on. SPIN, MEDDIC, command of the message — they're all variants of running a structured project.
topics.sourceQuote: When you realize that managing a sale is like a project. Man, it just like takes on a whole different meaning... how do you how do you master the art of psychology and working with lots of different folks... and then methodically managing your way through sales.

topics.title: Hire the Range Resume
topics.angle: Sales hires with eclectic, non-traditional backgrounds — supply chain, project management, military, ops — bring a methodical structure no career SDR has. Read the book Range and rewrite your hiring rubric.
topics.sourceQuote: I I love sales folks with non-traditional backgrounds. I think it brings a completely different perspective in like how do you methodically move through the sale?... I love the book range.

topics.title: The Soft-Skills Salesperson Trap
topics.angle: The most charismatic person in your sales pipeline review is rarely the top closer. Charisma without process discipline gets you great first meetings and zero late-stage deals.
topics.sourceQuote: I've seen plenty of people who have like all the soft skills, gifted gap, presentation, very, very you know, exciting people to be around that don't close anything because they're not moving the deal along as they need to.

topics.title: RevOps → CRO Is a Real Career Path
topics.angle: If you're in RevOps and want the CRO seat, you don't have to skip the AE chair. 18-24 months as an AE teaches you what no dashboard can: how a deal actually moves. Then go back to leadership.
topics.sourceQuote: I did take in it was a strategic account manager role in a software organization for like 21 months. And I kind of went in and I said, look, I want to do this to learn the product, learn the customer base, and then get back into leadership.

topics.title: Hands-Dirty Leadership Beats Pedigree
topics.angle: The fastest way to earn the respect of a sales team you've never sold for: do every uncomfortable thing you're asking them to do. Run accounts yourself. Make the calls. Sit in the demos. The team will see it within 30 days.
topics.sourceQuote: First of all, I've never been afraid of getting my hands dirty. So I would go manage accounts individually on my own... if you're always willing to get your hands dirty and do anything you ask your team to do.

topics.title: Customer-Facing Stints Are the Best RevOps Upgrade
topics.angle: Every RevOps person should spend a year customer-facing — AE, CSM, anything. The empathy, prioritization, and field-level pattern recognition you bring back makes you 10x more effective at the systems job.
topics.sourceQuote: Even if you don't take that and move into a CRO role, let's say you're in RevOps, you go do an AE role, you go do a customer success role, it's just anything customer facing... I think when you come back, you'll have more empathy, better recommendations, you'll be thinking about your customer better.

topics.title: The Vertical SaaS Account-Compression Trap
topics.angle: Shrinking territories to go deeper with strategic accounts is sound vertical SaaS strategy — until it's not. Compress too aggressively and even your top reps miss attainment. Then you have to walk it back without losing face.
topics.sourceQuote: We leaned into that a little bit, you know, earlier this year. And I thought we got a little bit aggressive on hiring. We restructured the territories, made them a little bit thinner. we pretty quickly pulled that back when we saw there was some like lack of attainment.

topics.title: Med Tech Hires Bring Industry, SaaS Hires Bring Process
topics.angle: In vertical SaaS, you don't pick between industry experts and SaaS veterans. You hire both and cross-pollinate. Domain folks ramp into process discipline; SaaS folks earn industry credibility. The blend is the moat.
topics.sourceQuote: We do have an awesome blend of people that have been selling software in the past and people that have been selling med tech in the past and have sort of cross-pollinated and learn the others' industry.

topics.title: Mission-Aligned Hires Outperform On Year-Two Retention
topics.angle: In altruistic-mission categories — healthcare, education, climate — the candidates who buy into the why outlast the ones chasing comp. Tell the mission story in interviews; it's a real qualifier, not fluff.
topics.sourceQuote: I think we've seen a lot of success for people that buy into what we're doing, really want to support their customers' mission. And it's kind of a unique position, I think, for us to have an altruistic mission as a software organization.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: The Three Stages of GTM Build
posts.postText:
**$2M to $50M ARR isn't one motion — it's three.**

Most founders try to install stage-three rigor in a stage-one company. Then they wonder why nothing sticks.

We put together a simple visual on the three stages of GTM build — what actually wins in each one, and where teams burn cycles trying to skip ahead.

0-10M is hand-to-hand combat.
10-25M is process layering.
25M+ is pipeline-metric sophistication.

Know what stage you're in. Stop running the wrong playbook.

Swipe through 👇
posts.voiceNotes: Bold thesis open with 'isn't just X — it's Y' construction. Em-dashes, short punchy lines. Closes with the signature pointing-down emoji and a swipe CTA — used once in the batch.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A 5-slide carousel walking through the three stages of GTM build from $2M to $50M ARR. Opens with a cover slide framing the thesis, then one slide per stage (hand-to-hand combat, process layering, pipeline-metric sophistication) with the dominant motion and the failure mode for each, and closes with a dark final slide delivering the payoff that most founders try to install stage-three rigor in a stage-one company.

params.title: The Three Stages of GTM Build
params.body: $2M to $50M ARR isn't one motion. It's three. Most founders run the wrong one for the stage they're in.

params.title: Hand-to-Hand Combat ($0-10M)
params.body: Founder-led deals. Excitement closes pipeline. You win on relationships, urgency, and willingness to do anything to land the logo. Process is a distraction here.

params.title: Process Layering ($10-25M)
params.body: Standardize the motion the second you have more than one rep. Stage gates, MEDDIC, forecast cadence, enablement. The energy that got you here will not scale you to $25M.

params.title: Pipeline-Metric Sophistication ($25M+)
params.body: Funnel conversion by segment. CAC payback by motion. Pipeline coverage by quarter, by rep, by source. You're now managing an engine, not a deal sheet.

params.title: Run the playbook for the stage you're in.
params.body: Stage-three rigor in a stage-one company kills momentum. Stage-one chaos in a stage-three company kills the engine. Diagnose, then build.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Reps Should Generate 50% of Their Own Pipe
posts.postText:
**Stop blaming SDRs and marketing for thin pipeline.**

The benchmark for a healthy field org: reps self-source roughly half their own primary pipeline. Your top reps will quietly outperform that number every quarter.

Why? Because more successful reps create more of their own pipe. It's not a coincidence. It's the leading indicator.

If your team is sitting at 15-20% self-sourced, the issue isn't inbound volume — it's a coaching and accountability gap. Fix that before you hire another BDR.

One image. Save it for your next QBR.
posts.voiceNotes: Bold thesis hook with 'Stop X' construction. 'Why?' pivot — straight from the sample phrasings. Plain operator language: pipeline, self-source, QBR, BDR. Closes with a clean utility line, no emoji.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A single image with a clear benchmark callout showing the 50% rep self-sourced pipeline benchmark as the centerpiece, with a short body explanation that top reps outperform this number and that low self-sourced numbers signal a coaching gap, not an inbound gap.

params.title: Reps Self-Source Half Their Pipe
params.statLabel: of a rep's primary pipeline should be self-sourced
params.body: Top reps outperform this number every quarter. If your team is at 15-20%, you don't have a marketing problem. You have a coaching and accountability gap.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Late RevOps Is the Tax You Pay Forever
posts.postText:
**Pattern across nearly every CRO conversation we have: they brought RevOps in too late.**

And the cost isn't bad dashboards.

The cost is 18+ months of compounding decisions made on no data — bad hires, bad territories, bad enablement spend, bad forecast calls. Every one of those is a tax you keep paying long after RevOps finally shows up.

I lived this myself. We didn't bring RevOps on until 2.5 years into the build. The catch-up work was brutal — and a lot of it was unwinding decisions we never should have made in the first place.

The rule we now give founders: the second you have more than one person doing sales, that's the moment to start standardizing. Earlier is almost always better.

RevOps isn't a back-office function. It's the operating system that decides whether you scale efficiently or burn equity.
posts.voiceNotes: INDUSTRY OBSERVATION shape — 'Pattern across...' setup straight from the voice profile. Pulls in his personal RevOps backstory as proof. Lands on a recurring thesis ('RevOps isn't a back-office function...') verbatim from his POV list. No emoji, no sign-off — clean payoff line.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: The AI-First Mental Wall
posts.postText:
**The blocker to AI adoption isn't tech literacy. It's the belief that software is hard.**

For twenty years we trained people to memorize dropdowns, menus, tab orders, and field hierarchies. "Knowing the software" was a skill.

AI doesn't add a new wave to learn — it removes the one you already learned.

No more dropdowns. No more guessing which tab holds the report. You ask for what you want, in the words you'd use, and the work happens.

Don't overthink it. Just ask.
posts.voiceNotes: CONTRARIAN HOOK shape — bold polarizing claim in line one, 'isn't X — it's Y' construction. Em-dashes. Short punchy close lifted directly from his voice ('Don't overthink it. Ask whatever you want.'). No emoji, no CTA stamp.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Don't Lay Off — Just Don't Over-Hire
posts.postText:
**Financially conservative GTM orgs don't fire well. They hire slowly.**

If you're a CRO and you've had to do a layoff, you already lost. The discipline lives 12 months earlier — in how aggressively you green-light the next hire.

Here's the playbook we run at LeanScale and recommend to portfolio CROs:

1. Build a ramped-quota headcount plan, not a flat one. Each rep has a month-by-month attainment ramp baked in.

2. Set an attainment threshold that triggers the next hire — e.g. "we only hire rep 9 when reps 1-8 collectively hit 85% of ramped quota for two consecutive quarters."

3. The moment attainment dips below threshold, pause hiring. Not slow it down — pause it. The plan does the work for you.

4. Run this monthly with finance. Not quarterly. The lag kills you.

5. Re-forecast headcount alongside pipeline coverage, not separately. They are the same conversation.

Layoffs are loud. Discipline is quiet. The quiet version compounds.
posts.voiceNotes: TACTICAL PLAYBOOK shape — numbered SOP, specific thresholds, named cadences. Opens with bold thesis. Uses 'Here's the playbook we run at LeanScale' construction lifted from his samples. Closes on an aphorism payoff, no emoji, no CTA — varies the close.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Selling Is Just Project Management With Psychology On Top
posts.postText:
The best sellers I've worked with at Fountain, AssemblyAI, and now LeanScale all share one trait — they treat the deal like a project plan with stakeholder management bolted on top.

SPIN, MEDDIC, Command of the Message — they're all variants of the same thing: a structured project with humans inside it.

Put together a quick visual on how we coach reps to think about complex deals. Swipe through 👇
posts.voiceNotes: Operator-to-operator framing with three customer/company name-drops (Fountain, AssemblyAI, LeanScale) per his pattern. Uses the 👇 pointer emoji he favors on carousel teasers. Tight 3-block structure, no contrarian bait.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Five-slide carousel teaching that complex B2B sales is project management plus stakeholder psychology — opens with the thesis, walks through the four project phases of a deal, lands on a quote-style payoff slide.

params.title: Selling Is Project Management With Psychology On Top
params.body: Every complex deal is a structured project. The reps who internalize that outperform the reps still treating sales as a personality contest.

params.title: Scope the project
params.body: Map the buying committee, decision criteria, and timeline. SPIN questions, MEDDIC fields — this is requirements gathering with a quota attached.

params.title: Build the plan
params.body: Mutual close plan, owners on both sides, dependencies surfaced. Replace 'next steps' with a Gantt chart your champion can defend internally.

params.title: Manage the humans
items: Economic buyer wants proof of ROI
Champion wants to look smart internally
End user wants the work to feel easier
Legal wants risk reduced to zero
Each stakeholder gets a tailored asset

params.title: Drive to close
params.body: Weekly status updates, blockers escalated, signatures sequenced. The rep is the project manager — accountable for the date, not just the relationship.

params.title: Methodology is the project plan
params.body: Pick your acronym — SPIN, MEDDIC, Command of the Message. They're all the same instinct: run the deal like a project, and let psychology shape how you communicate inside it.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Med Tech Hires Bring Industry, SaaS Hires Bring Process
posts.postText:
We've got an awesome blend of people on our customer GTM teams — half came up selling software, half came up selling med tech, and they've cross-pollinated into something neither side could build alone.

In vertical SaaS, it's not industry expert OR SaaS veteran. It's both, hired on purpose, ramped into each other's strengths.

Domain folks pick up process discipline. SaaS folks earn industry credibility. The blend is the moat.

#verticalSaaS #gtm
posts.voiceNotes: Leans on his plural 'we' framing and 'awesome blend' phrasing pulled directly from his sample vocabulary. Closes with a clean 1-2 hashtag bundle (his observed pattern) instead of a salute. Front-loads the thesis in the first two lines for the see-more cutoff.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single infographic with a side-by-side comparison: left column shows what industry hires (e.g., med tech sellers) bring to a vertical SaaS go-to-market, right column shows what SaaS-native hires bring, and a center band showing what emerges when they cross-pollinate.

params.title: Two Resumes, One Team
items: Industry hires bring buyer relationships and domain credibility
SaaS hires bring repeatable process and tooling discipline
Industry hires learn MEDDIC, forecasting cadence, CRM hygiene
SaaS hires learn the procurement quirks of the vertical
The cross-pollination becomes the moat competitors can't copy

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Curated > Volume in 2026 Outbound
posts.postText:
Curated outbound is beating volume outbound in 2026, and the math isn't even close. Here's the play we run with our B2B startup customers:

1. Pick 50 accounts you can credibly help this quarter. Not 5,000.

2. Research each one — funding round, recent hires, product launches, public pain. Clay + an LLM gets you 80% there in an hour.

3. Match the use case to something specific the buyer just said publicly. No generic value prop slop.

4. Record a 90-second Loom inside your own product, walking through the exact problem you'd solve for them.

5. Send it in a 4-line email. No PDFs, no calendar links in the opener.

A 2% blast-reply rate on 5,000 contacts gets you 100 cold conversations. 50 curated touches with a personalized Loom gets you 12 warm intro meetings. Different game entirely.
posts.voiceNotes: TACTICAL PLAYBOOK — his primary content type per the profile. Numbered SOP structure, named tool (Clay) per his tool-mention habit, concrete numbers throughout, no emoji, no engagement-bait close. Lands on a math comparison rather than a CTA.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Events Are Back — But Only If You Run Them Like a Sales Cycle
posts.postText:
Events stopped being boondoggles the moment teams started running them like a sales cycle.

Watched one of our B2B SaaS customers wrap a recent industry conference — 38 booked meetings, 22 stage-2 opps in CRM, 4 closed-won deals 90 days later. Sub-six-figure event spend.

The magic wasn't the booth. It was the operational wrap-around:

Pre-event — target list of 200 accounts, owners assigned, calendar invites sent two weeks out, hosted dinner waitlist live.

On-site — pre-cep meetings every morning, deal-cycle stage updates every night, swag tied to outcome (not pity).

Post-event — every conversation logged in Salesforce within 48 hours, follow-up sequences fired, AE/CSM/Partnerships joint accountability tracked to closure.

If you're running events without that wrap-around in 2026, you're funding a vacation. With it, you're funding a quarter.
posts.voiceNotes: CASE STUDY SNIPPET with concrete outcome numbers (38 meetings, 22 opps, 4 closed-won) matching his 'every claim backed with a number' avoidance rule. Salesforce name-drop, no emoji, no salute close — clean payoff line lands the post.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Hire the Range Resume
posts.postText:
Pattern we keep seeing across the highest-performing GTM teams we work with:

The top quartile of reps almost never have a clean career SDR-to-AE-to-Senior-AE resume.

They have eclectic backgrounds — supply chain, project management, military, consulting, ops. The kind of resumes career-sales hiring managers used to throw out for being 'unfocused.'

What they bring is methodical structure. They've moved a complex thing through multiple stakeholders before. They don't panic when a deal stalls in legal because they've watched 50 things stall in legal in a past life.

David Epstein's Range made the case better than I can — generalists with deep skills compound faster than specialists in messy, fast-changing fields. B2B sales is one of those fields.

If your hiring rubric still weights 'years selling SaaS' over 'years moving complex projects through humans,' update it.
posts.voiceNotes: INDUSTRY OBSERVATION using his observed 'Pattern we're seeing across...' opener (lifted from sample phrasings). Drops a specific book/author reference (Range, David Epstein) per his 'specifics over abstractions' rule. Direct prescription at the end, no emoji, no hashtag — pure payoff close to vary the sign-off pattern across the batch.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: RevOps → CRO Is a Real Career Path
posts.postText:
If you're in RevOps and you want the CRO seat someday, here's the move most people are too scared to make.

Go carry a bag for 18-24 months.

I did it. Took a Strategic Account Manager role at a software org for 21 months specifically so I could learn the product, the customer base, and what a deal actually feels like in the field — then move back into leadership.

No dashboard teaches you that.

Swipe through the path.
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with a conditional pain-point framing (not a contrarian claim) and downshifts into teacherly mode. Uses his calm, instructive register and personal-credibility move ('I did it'). Closes with a simple swipe CTA, no salute, no hashtags.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide swipeable carousel laying out the RevOps-to-CRO career path: cover slide, framework slide on why the AE detour matters, listicle of what you actually learn in the seat, stat callout on the 21-month timeline, dark closing slide with the core principle.

params.title: RevOps to CRO Is a Real Path
params.body: You don't have to skip the AE chair. In fact, you probably shouldn't.

params.title: Spend 18-24 months in a customer-facing seat
params.body: AE, SAM, CSM — pick one. Long enough to own a full cycle. Short enough to come back before you're typecast.

params.title: Things no dashboard can teach you
items: How a deal actually moves from stage to stage
What a buyer cares about vs. what your CRM thinks they care about
Where forecast slips really come from
Why reps push back on the process you designed
How fast a quarter disappears when you're carrying it

params.title: 21 months in the seat
params.statLabel: MONTHS AS A STRATEGIC ACCOUNT MANAGER
params.body: Took a SAM role at a software org with one goal: learn the product, learn the customer base, then go back to leadership.

params.title: Go carry the bag. Then come back and lead.
params.body: The CROs I trust most have all sat in the seat they're now coaching.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: The 6-8-10 Rep Rule for RevOps
posts.postText:
Founders ask me all the time: when do we actually hire RevOps?

Here's the simple trigger I give them.

The day you have 6-8-10 reps generating data, you need a full-time human whose only job is making that data trustworthy. Before that, the founder or the CRO can stitch it together with duct tape. After that, the gap compounds and you spend the next year trying to catch up.

Save this one for your next planning conversation.
posts.voiceNotes: Plain operator framing, no contrarian opener. Uses 'I give them' to ground it in his actual conversations. 'Duct tape' is gentle, not edgy. Closes with a soft save-it line — not a salute, not a hashtag.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single dense 1080x1080 infographic with a big stat callout of '6-8-10' over the label 'reps generating data,' a one-line setup above, and 2-3 short supporting lines underneath explaining the before/after trigger.

params.title: When to hire your first RevOps lead
params.body: Before that, the founder or CRO can stitch the data. After that, you need a full-time RevOps hire or the gap compounds for a year.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Hire RevOps for Dirt, Not Dashboards
posts.postText:
When you write the RevOps job description, put 'data janitor' in the first paragraph.

I know — RevOps sounds glamorous. Dashboards. Forecasting. Strategic partner to the CRO.

But the best hires I've ever seen want to get their hands dirty in CRM hygiene first. They know you can't build a forecast on a Salesforce instance that nobody trusts.

A few things I now say out loud in every interview:

- Year one is data cleanliness. We are not building a Looker dashboard on day one.
- You will own field hygiene, stage definitions, and dedupe before you own strategy.
- The foundation is the job. The dashboards come once it exists.

The candidates who light up at that are the ones who turn into great RevOps leaders.

The ones who deflate were never going to enjoy the work.
posts.voiceNotes: Tactical playbook in teacherly mode — opens with a concrete recommendation, then explains it. Bulleted middle matches his long-form pattern. Lands quietly, no CTA stamp.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Customer-Facing Stints Are the Best RevOps Upgrade
posts.postText:
Best thing I ever did for my RevOps career was take a year off from RevOps.

I moved into a customer-facing seat — owning a book of accounts, running my own pipeline, sitting across from buyers who didn't care about my data model.

What I brought back was hard to put on a resume.

More empathy for the rep who can't fit one more required field on the opp.

A better instinct for which projects actually move revenue and which ones just look productive on a roadmap.

Pattern recognition I couldn't have built from a Salesforce report — the way a deal stalls, the way a customer signals churn before the NPS does, the way a forecast call really gets made.

I came back 10x more effective at the systems job. And honestly, a much better teammate.

If you're in RevOps and you've never carried a number or owned a customer, that's the upgrade I'd push you toward next. Doesn't have to be forever. A year is enough.
posts.voiceNotes: Personal story in reflective mode — matches his anniversary-post rhythm of short paragraphs and one idea per line. No bullets. Lands on quiet advice, not a CTA stamp.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Vertical SaaS Hiring: Mirror Your Customer
posts.postText:
Pattern I keep seeing in vertical SaaS hiring:

The slickest SaaS sellers are almost never the right hires.

The winners are the ones whose work ethic mirrors the customers they're selling to.

If your buyers are operators who grind — restaurant owners, contractors, clinic managers — you need grinders. People who will sit on the phone, show up early, and learn the workflow.

If your buyers are mission-driven — nonprofits, schools, healthcare — you hire for mission alignment over pedigree. The rep who genuinely cares about the customer outcome will outsell the rep with a fancier logo on their resume every time.

In vertical SaaS, the customer is the filter. The job is to find people who want to become experts in that world and put in the work to support customers doing real work.

Mirror the customer base. The rest follows.
posts.voiceNotes: Industry observation in his calm, pattern-recognition voice. Uses his 'mirror your customer' construction. No emoji, no hashtags, lands on the principle.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Decentralize Experimentation, Centralize Production
posts.postText:
Every employee should be building their own AI hacks.

And the moment one of those hacks becomes a process the org depends on, RevOps owns it.

Skip that handoff and you have a bowl of spaghetti within six months — 40 half-broken Zapiers, three rogue GPTs, and nobody who can tell you which one is touching the CRM.

The model we're moving toward at LeanScale: decentralize experimentation, centralize production.

Swipe through the build order.
posts.voiceNotes: Stat-free reframe hook (his second-favorite style), em-dash punch, parallel structure 'decentralize X, centralize Y' lifted from his sample phrasings rhythm. Closes with a swipe-CTA, no salute or hashtag stamp.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel walking through a two-mode AI operating model: individual experimentation on the left, RevOps-owned production on the right, with a clear handoff trigger in the middle and a closing principle.

params.title: Decentralize experimentation. Centralize production.
params.body: How to let every employee build with AI without your stack turning into spaghetti in six months.

params.title: Decentralized experimentation
params.body: Every employee builds their own hacks for their own work. Prompts, GPTs, small automations. Encourage it. No approval queue.

params.title: Centralized production
params.body: The moment a hack becomes a process the org depends on, RevOps owns the rebuild. One source of truth. One on-call team.

params.title: Move it to RevOps when:
items: More than one person runs it weekly
It writes to a system of record (CRM, billing, comp)
A customer outcome depends on it working
The builder is the only one who can fix it

params.title: Experimentation everywhere. Production in one place.
params.body: Anything else is a bowl of spaghetti by month six. We've seen it inside our own walls before we wrote the rule.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Top 5 Paid = Top 5 Reps. That's the Test.
posts.postText:
One question to diagnose whether your GTM org is healthy:

Are the top 5 paid people in your company the top 5 sales reps?

If yes — your incentive structure is doing its job. The people closest to revenue are getting paid the most, which means the people closest to revenue are the ones you'll keep.

If no — and it's a VP, a director, a manager, a founder draw, anyone else sitting in those top 5 slots — your comp design is fighting your growth.

It's the cleanest test I know. One image below.
posts.voiceNotes: Question-as-hook with immediate reframe, his 'cleanest test I know' tone — confident, understated, no hype. Closes with a pointer to the visual, no salute or hashtag stamp.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single stat slide leading with the headline test, a short list of what it means when the answer is yes versus no, and a quiet credit line.

params.title: Top 5 paid in your company = top 5 sales reps?
items: YES → Incentive structure is working. Revenue earners are your highest earners.
NO → A VP, director, or founder is in a slot a closer should own.
NO → Your comp plan is quietly competing with your growth plan.
NO → Expect your best reps to leave before your next board meeting.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: GTM-Side AI Adoption Is Outrunning Eng/Product
posts.postText:
Pattern we're seeing across the portfolio that runs counter to every dev-team-first assumption:

RevOps and CS teams are adopting AI faster than engineering and product teams.

Not a little faster. Visibly faster.

The reason is muscle memory. Engineers have years of how-they-build encoded into their workflow — IDEs, code review rituals, testing patterns. AI threatens to rewrite all of that, and rewriting takes time even when the rewrite is better.

Go-to-market teams don't have that legacy. A CSM building an AI workflow today isn't unlearning anything — they're skipping straight to the new way.

The teams without legacy workflows are leapfrogging the teams with them. Including, in many cases, the engineers who built the products those GTM teams sell.
posts.voiceNotes: Classic 'pattern we're seeing' opener from his sample phrasings, calm reframe energy, parallel structure on the why. Closes with a clean declarative restatement of the thesis — no CTA, no emoji.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Pivot to AI-Native Means Eating Some Growth
posts.postText:
There is no painless way to become AI-native.

You get two doors:

Door 1 — rip the bandage off. Retool the org, kill the workflows that don't survive contact with AI, ship the new motion this quarter. You will get killed on revenue this year. The next two years look different.

Door 2 — slow burn it. Protect the growth curve. Phase the transition over 18-24 months. You stay healthy, you stay funded, you arrive at the same destination two years later than the founder who picked Door 1.

There is no Door 3 where you transform the company AND post your best year ever. Anyone selling you that is selling you something.

Pick your tradeoff on purpose, not by accident.
posts.voiceNotes: Contrarian-but-constructive frame, opens with the claim itself (no 'Hot take:' label — his avoidance). Door framing keeps it tactile. Closes with a clean declarative — no salute, no question bait.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The 20% Pickup Rate Most Teams Will Never Get
posts.postText:
AcuityMD is hitting 20% phone pickup rates and 12-13% email response rates on outbound.

For context: most B2B teams celebrate 3-5% on either.

The reason is not the tooling. It's the shape of the motion.

They sell vertical — a narrow buyer in a specific corner of medtech. Every rep knows the named accounts cold. Every message is curated, not templated. The list is small enough that 'who am I writing to' is a real answer, not a persona.

The takeaway for everyone else:

Spray-and-pray outbound is dead — except for the lowest-stakes commodity sale where volume genuinely beats precision. For everything in between, the AcuityMDs of the world are eating your reply rate while your reps send 200 sequences a day.

Narrow the buyer. Curate the message. Or accept the 3%.
posts.voiceNotes: Case-study-with-named-company hook, his structured contrast pattern ('predictably good when X, predictably bad when not' echoed in 'narrow + curated vs spray-and-pray'). Closes with a tight three-line payoff — no CTA, no emoji.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: The Spaghetti Phase Is a Real Phase
posts.postText:
Every company that hands its team Claude Code goes through the same three-phase arc.

We lived it ourselves at LeanScale Labs. First came the creative explosion — everyone building something. Then the bowl of spaghetti — 40 half-finished tools, no shared patterns, no one quite sure what's in production.

You cannot skip the spaghetti phase. You can only shorten it.

Put together a quick visual on the arc and the levers that compress it. Swipe through.
posts.voiceNotes: Show-don't-shout opener — names the artifact (we lived it at LeanScale Labs) instead of leading with a take. Uses 'cannot skip / can only shorten' construction that fits his declarative builder rhythm. Closes with 'Swipe through' — straight from his sample phrasings. No emojis, no hashtags.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel walking through the predictable arc every company experiences when rolling out Claude Code internally — creativity explosion, spaghetti chaos, forced consolidation — and the levers that shorten the messy middle.

params.title: The Spaghetti Phase Is a Real Phase
params.body: What happens when you give every employee Claude Code — and how to shorten the chaos in the middle.

params.title: Creative Explosion
params.body: Every engineer, AE, and ops person ships something in week one. Internal tools, scrapers, agents, dashboards. Energy is real.

params.title: Bowl Of Spaghetti
params.body: Forty half-finished tools. No shared patterns. Three people built the same lead scorer. Nobody can say what's in production.

params.title: Forced Consolidation
params.body: Someone draws a map. Tools get retired, merged, or promoted. A platform team emerges. The leverage starts compounding.

params.title: Four Levers That Compress Phase Two
items: Weekly internal demo day — surface duplicates fast
One shared repo of approved patterns and prompts
Name one engineer as the platform editor
Kill anything no human has opened in 30 days

params.title: You Cannot Skip It. You Can Only Shorten It.
params.body: Spaghetti is the cost of giving real builders real tools. The companies that win compress the middle on purpose.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Hire the Range Resume
posts.postText:
Some of the best sales hires we've seen at our clients did not come up through the SDR-to-AE pipeline.

Supply chain. Project management. Military logistics. Operations. People who have already learned how to methodically move a complex thing from A to B with a real plan and real checkpoints.

They bring structure a career SDR was never asked to build. They run a deal the way they ran a project — milestones, risks, owners, dates.

If you have not read David Epstein's Range, read it. Then rewrite your hiring rubric.

One visual on the kind of background pattern I'd weight up.
posts.voiceNotes: Earnest, plain-spoken, builder-pragmatic. Opens with a calm observation (not a hot take), gives the reasoning in declarative medium-length sentences, lands on a soft directive ('read it. Then rewrite your hiring rubric'). Cites Range by name — concrete reference. Closes with a one-liner pointing at the artifact, matching his show-don't-shout default.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single-slide infographic showing the kinds of non-traditional backgrounds that produce strong methodical sales hires, framed as a hiring rubric prompt.

params.title: Backgrounds I'd Weight Up On A Sales Slate
items: Supply chain or logistics planner
Project or program manager (PMP energy)
Military officer or NCO with ops command
Field engineer turned commercial
Operations lead from a complex industry
Anything that ran on milestones and risk logs

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Your Customer-Facing Team Must Be Power Users of Your Own AI
posts.postText:
If you shipped an AI feature in your product, your customer-facing team has to use it every day. Not demo it. Use it.

The Sherpa model is the only one I see actually move adoption past the launch press release.

How we are running it inside LeanScale, with about 80 customer-facing people:

1. Every CSM, AE, and SE has the AI features on in their own workflow, not just in the demo org.
2. Weekly office hours — internal team teaches internal team. No vendor required.
3. One internal Slack channel for prompts that worked. One for prompts that did not. Both get read.
4. Adoption metric is internal first, customer second. If our own team is not in it daily, we do not show it to a customer.
5. CSM-led customer trainings replace the recorded webinar.

The gap between shipping a feature and a customer using it is the Sherpa.

What is your team doing to close it?
posts.voiceNotes: TACTICAL PLAYBOOK — Jake's primary lane. Setup line states the problem, then numbered SOP-style steps that are concrete and specific. Uses 'how we are running it' construction that's straight from his sample phrasings. Soft practitioner question at the end, not a sales CTA. Medium length (~210 words). No emojis.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Hands-Dirty Leadership Beats Pedigree
posts.postText:
I have never sold software. I run engineering and product.

The first time I had to win the respect of a sales team that did not report to anyone like me, I did not pitch them my background. I did not have one that mattered to them.

What I did was manage accounts. Personally. I picked up two of the messiest ones on the board, ran the calls, sat in the demos, wrote the follow-ups. I did every uncomfortable thing I was about to ask them to do.

It took roughly 30 days.

After that, when I asked someone to make a hard call or rework a workflow they had been protecting for two years, the answer was different. Because they had already watched me do the harder version of it.

The shortcut to credibility with an operating team you have not earned a seat with is to do the work in front of them. Pedigree does not travel across functions. Hands-dirty does.

It is the only leadership move I trust when I am the new one in the room.
posts.voiceNotes: PERSONAL STORY, but in Jake's restrained register — not vulnerability-bait, not a long arc. First-person, specific (two messiest accounts, 30 days), lands on a clean operating principle. Uses 'roughly 30 days' — Jake's concrete-number habit. Closes on a quiet payoff, no CTA, no emoji. Stays inside his 200-300 word zone for a story post.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: The Vertical SaaS Account-Compression Trap
posts.postText:
Saw this play out at a vertical SaaS company earlier this year. Useful lesson if you are running a similar motion.

The strategy was sound on paper. Vertical SaaS rewards depth, so they shrank territories — fewer accounts per rep, more strategic focus, theoretically higher penetration per logo.

They leaned into it. Hired ahead. Restructured the territory map. Cut average accounts per rep by roughly a third.

The break showed up in the next quarter. Even their top reps started missing attainment. Not because the accounts were worse — because the math no longer worked. Pipeline coverage dropped below what any of them needed to clear quota, and the strategic-account work takes a quarter or two to convert.

They pulled it back. Quietly widened the books. Kept the strategic overlay where it actually was working and unwound it where it was not.

Lesson I took from it — account compression is the right call when you are confident the conversion rate per account climbs faster than coverage falls. If you are not sure, run it on one segment first. Walking back a territory cut without losing the room is harder than designing the test that prevents it.
posts.voiceNotes: CASE STUDY SNIPPET — Jake's other primary lane. Anonymized customer, specific intervention, specific result, generalizable lesson. Uses 'roughly a third' and 'quarter or two' — his concrete-number habit. Lands on an operating principle, not a sales pitch. Stays inside his medium-length window. No emoji, no signature closer (carousel already used one) — clean payoff line.
